content.text: Why Standard PRD Templates Fall Short for GenAI Features

A standard software PRD organises requirements around screens, user stories, and data models. A GenAI feature PRD must organise requirements around four fundamentally different concerns: what the agent is supposed to do (capability specification), how it is supposed to behave in dialogue (conversation behaviour), how its quality and safety will be measured (eval and risk), and what infrastructure and operational constraints govern its deployment (delivery). A template built for deterministic software will miss all four of these concerns and produce a PRD that leaves engineers guessing about the most important implementation decisions.

This sub-subtopic presents a sixteen-section PRD template designed specifically for agentic AI features. Each section is mandatory — not because a template demands completeness for its own sake, but because each section feeds a specific downstream artifact (backlog stories, eval scenarios, prompt requirements, or launch criteria) that cannot be correctly authored without it.
The Sixteen-Section Structure

The sixteen sections cluster into four groups: Foundation (sections 1–4) establishes the business context and scope. Behaviour Specification (sections 5–8) defines what the agent does and says. Quality and Safety (sections 9–12) defines how the agent's output will be measured and bounded. Delivery and Operations (sections 13–16) defines infrastructure, observability, and launch criteria.
Section 1: Job Statement

The job statement is a single sentence in job-story format: "When [trigger], I want to [action], so I can [outcome]." It anchors the entire PRD. Every subsequent section must trace back to this statement — if a capability, a prompt requirement, or an eval scenario cannot be justified by the job statement, it should not be in the PRD.

A weak job statement is: "The agent will help users with invoice management." A strong one is: "When I receive an invoice that needs approval, I want to check its current status without leaving my email client, so I can approve or escalate it immediately without opening the ERP system." The strong version specifies the trigger (invoice needing approval), the context constraint (without leaving email), and the outcome precision (approve or escalate immediately).
Section 2: Personas and Segments

List the primary and secondary personas the feature serves. For each persona, include: the persona ID (cross-referenced to the persona development artifact), the key AI-specific attributes relevant to this feature (accuracy expectations, trust level, data sensitivity tolerance), and the persona's frequency and intensity of use. Frequency-intensity pairs drive eval scenario volume: a daily power user requires a larger eval suite than an occasional user because the cumulative risk exposure is higher.

Also list explicitly excluded segments. If the invoice status agent is not intended for external suppliers — only internal employees — state that here. An explicit exclusion prevents scope creep during implementation and makes the out-of-domain failure mode concrete: the agent should not attempt to serve a supplier who contacts it through the same channel.
Section 3: Success Metrics
GenAI feature success metrics fall into three categories: task completion metrics, quality metrics, and user experience metrics.
Task completion metrics: task completion rate (percentage of sessions where the agent fully resolves the user's job), median turn count to resolution, and abandonment rate at each journey stage.
Quality metrics: eval pass rate against the eval suite (target threshold specified here), factual accuracy rate on domain-specific questions, and rate of safety-policy violations.
User experience metrics: user satisfaction score (post-session survey), rate of human escalation requests (a proxy for trust loss), and response latency at p50, p95, and p99.

Each metric must have: a baseline (what the current non-AI experience achieves), a target (what the feature must achieve to be considered successful), and a measurement method (where the data comes from). Metrics without measurement methods are aspirations, not requirements.
Section 4: Scope and Out-of-Scope

The scope section is a two-column table. Left column: in-scope capabilities. Right column: explicitly out-of-scope capabilities. The out-of-scope column is not a polite way of saying "maybe later" — it is an instruction to the agent's system prompt designer and the engineer building the intent classifier. Out-of-scope items must be handled by the agent explicitly (graceful refusal and alternative routing), not silently ignored.

A common mistake is listing only in-scope items and leaving the out-of-scope column empty. This is dangerous because it leaves boundary behaviour undefined. When a user asks the invoice agent to also generate a new invoice, should it try and fail gracefully, or should it have never been capable of attempting? Explicitly out-of-scoping "invoice creation" in section 4 gives the prompt engineer a clear instruction and gives QA a test category.
Section 5: Agent Capability Map
The capability map lists every action the agent can take, organised by type: information retrieval, data mutation, external tool invocation, and dialogue management. For each capability:
Name and description: what the capability does in plain language.
Trigger condition: what user intent or context activates this capability.
Required inputs: what information the agent needs before it can execute the capability (may require slot-filling from prior turns).
Output: what the agent returns to the user on successful execution.
Side effects: any state changes in external systems (record updated, email sent, transaction processed).
Reversibility: can the action be undone? Irreversible actions require explicit user confirmation before execution.

The reversibility field is particularly important. Any capability with side effects that cannot be undone must be flagged here, because it drives both the conversation design (the agent must confirm before acting) and the eval design (an eval scenario must verify the agent requests confirmation).
Section 6: Conversational Journey

This section includes the conversational journey map produced during the requirements workshop (see the Journey Mapping sub-topic). The PRD does not re-author the map — it embeds it by reference or includes it as an appendix, and summarises the key design decisions it encodes: the five journey stages, the identified failure branches, the friction scores for each stage, and the memory requirements extracted from the map.

The journey map's failure branches are the direct input to section 10 (Failure Mode Register). Documenting this traceability in the PRD — "failure branches from journey map section 6 are catalogued in failure register section 10" — ensures that reviewers can verify the register is complete.
Section 7: Prompt Requirements
The prompt requirements section does not contain the actual system prompt — that belongs in the prompt registry and is versioned separately. It specifies what the system prompt must achieve:
Persona instruction: the voice, tone, and persona the agent must maintain (e.g., professional and concise; never humorous about financial matters).

Scope enforcement: instructions for refusing out-of-scope requests gracefully, with suggested alternative routing ("I can help with invoice status. For billing disputes, please contact accounts@company.com").
Safety constraints: topics the agent must never engage with, phrasings to avoid, and escalation triggers (e.g., if the user expresses distress, do not continue the task — offer support resources).

Clarification strategy: how many clarification attempts before escalation, the maximum number of turns the agent should spend in slot-filling, and whether the agent should make reasonable assumptions after exhausting clarification attempts.
Citation and uncertainty: how the agent should express uncertainty, when to cite sources, and when to acknowledge knowledge boundaries rather than generating an answer.
Section 8: Tool Definitions

List every external tool the agent will call: the tool name, the purpose, the input schema, the output schema, and the error schema. Include the timeout budget (how long the agent will wait before declaring a tool-call failure) and the fallback behaviour (what the agent does when the tool fails or times out).

The tool definitions in the PRD are design-level, not implementation-level. They specify what the tool must provide, not how it is implemented. This is the contract that the backend engineer uses to design the tool's API, and the conversation designer uses to write the agent's fallback responses.
Section 9: Eval Scenarios

The eval scenarios section contains at minimum: the number of planned scenarios, the scenario categories (happy path, failure mode, edge case, adversarial), and a representative sample of five to ten scenarios in structured format. The full scenario set is maintained in the eval suite repository and cross-referenced here.

Each scenario in the sample must include: a unique ID, the persona it represents, the input (user utterance or conversation transcript), the expected output (what the agent should say or do), the evaluation method (exact match, semantic similarity, human review, or automated classifier), and the failure mode it covers (cross-referenced to section 10).
Sections 10–16: Quality, Safety, and Delivery

Section 10 (Failure Mode Register) summarises the failure register produced during failure mode analysis, with counts by severity level and confirmation that all Functional and above failures have assigned mitigations.

Section 11 (Safety and Ethics) documents the specific safety policy the agent operates under: prohibited content categories, escalation triggers for safety concerns, bias considerations for the user population, and any regulatory requirements (GDPR, HIPAA, financial regulations) that constrain the agent's data handling and advice-giving behaviour.

Section 12 (Data and Privacy) lists the data the agent accesses, the data it stores (conversation logs, slot values, user preferences), the retention policy for each data type, and the access control model. In EU-regulated contexts, this section must also address the lawful basis for processing personal data under GDPR.

Section 13 (Model and Infrastructure Constraints) specifies the model provider, model version or version policy, maximum latency budget by scenario type (real-time chat has a tighter budget than batch analysis), and cost ceiling per session or per 1,000 sessions. This section prevents scope creep in the opposite direction: engineers should not choose a slower, cheaper model if the PRD specifies a latency requirement the model cannot meet.

Section 14 (Observability Requirements) lists the metrics, traces, and log events that must be emitted for the feature to be operationally manageable. At minimum: per-session latency, per-session token cost, intent classification distribution, tool call success and failure rates, and the eval suite pass rate on the production traffic sample. These requirements drive the engineering implementation of the observability layer.

Section 15 (Launch Gate Criteria) is a checklist. Every item must be checked before the feature ships: eval pass rate above threshold, all Functional+ failure modes mitigated, safety review completed, data privacy review completed, observability verified in staging, human escalation path tested end-to-end, rollout plan (percentage of users or traffic to start with) documented and approved.

Section 16 (Dependencies and Risks) lists external dependencies (third-party APIs, model provider availability, data pipeline freshness) and project risks (model regression risk, data schema change risk, team capacity risk). Each risk has an owner, a probability estimate, an impact estimate, and a contingency plan.
Writing the PRD: Common Mistakes

Leaving sections 9–16 as placeholders. The quality, safety, and delivery sections are the hardest to write and the easiest to defer. Teams that defer them past the first sprint planning session discover in sprint 3 that they have no eval suite, no observability plan, and no launch criteria — and the sprint is consumed by unplanned safety work.

Writing prompt requirements as prose instructions rather than structured constraints. "The agent should be helpful and professional" is not a prompt requirement. "The agent must use second-person address, avoid jargon, limit responses to three sentences for status queries, and never use the word 'unfortunately'" is a prompt requirement.
Omitting the out-of-scope column in section 4. An undefined boundary is an invitation for the agent to attempt behaviours the team has not designed or tested, producing unpredictable results.

Not cross-referencing sections. A PRD where sections 6, 9, and 10 are not explicitly connected — journey map failure branches feed the failure register, which feeds eval scenarios — will drift after the first sprint as the journey map is updated but the register and eval suite are not.
PRD Review and Sign-Off

A GenAI feature PRD requires sign-off from more stakeholders than a standard PRD, because more disciplines are affected. Minimum sign-off requirements: product owner (sections 1–4 and 15), conversation designer (sections 5–7), ML or AI engineer (sections 8, 13), security and privacy (sections 11–12), and a designated AI safety reviewer (sections 10–11 and 15).

Run the review in two passes. First pass: is every section complete? Empty or placeholder sections are returned to the author before the second pass begins. Second pass: are the requirements internally consistent? The job statement in section 1 must be satisfiable by the capabilities in section 5. The success metrics in section 3 must be measurable by the observability plan in section 14. The launch gate in section 15 must include the safety review required by section 11. Inconsistencies discovered in review are far cheaper than inconsistencies discovered in sprint 3.
Full PRD template example
The following skeleton shows how the sixteen sections map to a concrete GenAI feature: an AI-powered contract review assistant embedded in a legal workflow platform.

# PRD: Contract Review Assistant — GenAI Feature

## 1. Job Statement
When a legal associate receives a vendor contract,
they want to surface non-standard clauses and flag risk items,
so they can reduce manual review time from 4h to under 45 min.

## 2. Personas and Segments
- Primary: Legal associate (50–200 contracts/month, firm of 10–500 attorneys)
- Secondary: Partner (approval authority, reads summaries only)
- Trust level: Internal — full document access permitted

## 3. Success Metrics
| Metric | Baseline | Target | Measurement |
|--------|----------|--------|-------------|
| Review time (p50) | 4h | 45 min | Jira time-in-status |
| Clause recall | n/a | ≥ 95 % | Eval suite (200 labeled contracts) |
| False positive rate | n/a | < 5 % | Weekly human audit |
| NPS (associates) | 32 | ≥ 50 | Quarterly survey |

## 4. Scope
In scope: MSA, NDA, SaaS subscription agreements.
Out of scope: Employment contracts, IP assignments, M&A (Phase 2).

## 5. Agent Capability Map
- Tool: extract_clauses(doc_id) → JSON array of clause objects
- Tool: lookup_playbook(clause_type) → firm's standard position
- Tool: flag_deviation(clause, playbook_position) → risk_level ∈ {low, medium, high}
- Human in the loop: partner review required for high-risk flags before filing.

## 6. Conversational Journey
Turn 1 — User uploads PDF; agent acknowledges and begins extraction (streaming).
Turn 2 — Agent returns clause summary with inline citations.
Turn 3 — User asks "why is clause 12.3 flagged?"; agent explains deviation.
Turn 4 — User requests redline suggestions; agent generates DOCX diff.

## 7. Prompt Requirements
- System prompt must specify jurisdiction (US, UK, EU) at session start.
- Confidentiality: no clause text to be logged; only clause_type and risk_level.
- Response format: structured JSON for programmatic consumption; human summary optional.
- Tone: formal legal register; no hedging language ("may", "might") in risk flags.

## 8. Tool Definitions
extract_clauses:
  description: Extracts and classifies all clauses from a legal document.
  parameters:
    doc_id: string (UUID, references uploaded PDF)
    jurisdiction: enum [US, UK, EU]
  returns: [{clause_id, clause_type, text_excerpt, page, section}]
  latency_sla: p95 < 8 s (document ≤ 50 pages)

## 9. Eval Scenarios
- Happy path: 40-page MSA with 3 non-standard clauses → recall ≥ 95 %
- Edge: Scanned PDF with low OCR quality → graceful degradation, not hallucination
- Adversarial: Prompt injection in contract body → agent ignores, logs attempt
- Long doc: 200-page agreement → response within 30 s, no timeout
Sections 10–16 detail guide
The second half of the PRD addresses production readiness, safety, and delivery. Each section has a set of required fields that must be filled before the feature enters engineering sprint planning.
Section 10 — Hallucination & grounding policy
: specify which claims require citation, the retrieval corpus version, and the staleness threshold (e.g., index refreshed every 24 h).
Section 11 — Fallback and degraded modes
: define what happens when the LLM API is unavailable (cached last response, human escalation, or feature disable).
Section 12 — Data privacy and retention
: document what is logged (inputs, outputs, metadata), retention period, and deletion SLA to comply with GDPR/CCPA.
Section 13 — Bias and fairness assessment
: list protected attributes relevant to the use case and the eval datasets used to detect performance disparity.
Section 14 — Monitoring and alerting plan
: name the dashboards, alert owners, and on-call runbooks. Include the latency and error-rate SLOs.
Section 15 — Rollout and flag strategy
: describe canary percentages, feature-flag keys, kill-switch owner, and rollback criteria.
Section 16 — Open questions and dependencies
: table of unresolved decisions, owner, and deadline. Must be empty before engineering kick-off.
PRD review checklist
Before the PRD exits the product phase gate, every item below must be checked by the PM, lead engineer, and ML engineer jointly.

## GenAI PRD Gate Checklist

### Completeness
- [ ] All 16 sections filled (no TBD stubs)
- [ ] At least 20 labeled eval scenarios in Section 9
- [ ] Tool schemas in Section 8 reviewed by API team

### Safety and Compliance
- [ ] Privacy review completed (DPA attached)
- [ ] Bias assessment in Section 13 signed off by ethics lead
- [ ] Prompt injection mitigations described in Section 7

### Delivery Readiness
- [ ] Latency SLOs agreed with SRE (Section 14)
- [ ] Feature flag key registered in LaunchDarkly (Section 15)
- [ ] Rollback runbook linked (Section 15)
- [ ] Open questions table is empty (Section 16)

### Stakeholder Sign-Off
- [ ] Product Manager
- [ ] Engineering Lead
- [ ] ML / AI Platform Lead
- [ ] Legal / Compliance (if PII involved)
- [ ] Security (if external-facing)
Integrating the PRD into agile workflow

The GenAI PRD feeds three downstream artefacts: the technical design document (architecture decisions), the eval harness (test scenarios from Section 9), and the monitoring runbook (alert definitions from Section 14). Keeping these documents linked — via a shared ticket or wiki hierarchy — prevents sections from drifting out of sync as the feature evolves.
Pin the PRD version at each sprint boundary so retrospectives can compare delivered behaviour against the original requirements.
When the model or prompt changes mid-sprint, require a lightweight PRD amendment rather than an informal Slack agreement — this creates an audit trail.
Review Section 9 eval scenarios at sprint demo to confirm the eval suite ran and results are within the agreed tolerances.
Archive the PRD alongside the shipped feature's model card so future teams understand the original design intent.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role in PRD workflow
Notes
Confluence / Notion
PRD authoring and versioning
Lock edits after gate sign-off
Jira / Linear
Epic and story tracking
Link PRD URL in epic description
GitHub / GitLab
Eval suite code reviewed against Section 9
PR template includes PRD section checklist
LangSmith / Braintrust
Eval scenario management (Section 9)
Import scenarios from PRD via CSV
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses for GenAI
Standard software PRD
Familiar, tooling support
No prompt, eval, or hallucination fields
One-page spec
Fast, low overhead
Too thin for safety and compliance traceability
ML model card
Strong on model provenance
Not product-centric; no UX or rollout sections
GenAI 16-section PRD (this guide)
Full coverage: UX, evals, safety, delivery
Higher up-front authoring cost
Security and reliability considerations
Prompt injection vectors:
Section 7 must specify input sanitisation rules and which tool calls are available to the model. Never allow the model to call privileged tools from untrusted input.
Data leakage:
Section 12 must state whether PII or confidential content is sent to the LLM API and whether a data-processing agreement is in place with the provider.
Cascading failures:
Section 11 must define circuit-breaker thresholds so a degraded LLM API does not take down the entire product surface.
Supply-chain risk:
Pin the model version in the PRD; unplanned model updates have broken production evals for teams that relied on floating aliases.
Summary

A GenAI feature PRD is a sixteen-section document that organises requirements across four concerns: foundation, behaviour specification, quality and safety, and delivery. Each section is mandatory because each feeds a specific downstream artifact — backlog stories, eval scenarios, prompt requirements, or launch criteria — that cannot be correctly authored without it. A complete, internally consistent PRD with full stakeholder sign-off is the precondition for a sprint 1 that spends its capacity on building, not on resolving specification ambiguity.
